  2. Join the community: The Discord has active discussions on implementation challenges
  3. Try the examples: Clone the companion repository and run the code

The Bigger Picture

AI agents are moving from demos to production. The companies that will win aren’t those with the most sophisticated models—they’re the ones who can ship reliable, maintainable agent systems.

This book provides the foundation. Not by prescribing specific tools, but by teaching the thinking that makes good agent architectures possible.

“Principles of Building AI Agents” isn’t the only resource you’ll need. But it’s the one that helps everything else make sense.

References

  • Principles of Building AI Agents - Official Book
  • Announcing the Second Edition
  • Mastra.ai - TypeScript AI Agent Framework
  • Amazon Physical Copy
  • Sam Bhagwat on X (@calcsam)
  • Sumanth on X (@Sumanth_077)

item.title: Google Antigravity Terminal Sandboxing: AI Agents Finally Won't Delete Your Files
item.content:encoded:
Google Antigravity Terminal Sandboxing: AI Agents Finally Won’t Delete Your Files

Google Antigravity has just rolled out a terminal sandbox feature that addresses a very practical problem in agentic AI development: AI agents executing commands in the terminal will no longer accidentally mess with your system files.

The Problem AI Agents Created

When you give an AI agent terminal access, powerful things become possible. The agent can install dependencies, run tests, build projects, and deploy code. But this power comes with risk.

A simple mistake could lead to:

  • Accidentally deleting system files
  • Modifying configurations outside the project
  • Breaking your development environment
  • Running commands in unintended directories

This isn’t hypothetical—developers have experienced these issues firsthand when trusting autonomous agents with unrestricted terminal access.

How the Sandbox Works

The terminal sandbox employs kernel-level isolation technology, strictly confining all AI agent terminal commands within the project working directory.

As Google’s documentation states:

“When sandboxing is enabled, the Agent’s terminal commands are subject to the following restrictions: File System: Commands can only write to your designated workspace directory and essential system locations.”

Technical Implementation

Currently, this feature is implemented on macOS through Apple’s Seatbelt mechanism (sandbox-exec), providing kernel-level sandboxing. Linux support is planned for future releases.

The sandbox creates a designated playground for AI — it can only operate within the folder you specify. Network access can also be controlled independently, giving you fine-grained control over what your agent can do.

Enabling the Feature

The sandbox is currently opt-in (though this may change in future releases):

  1. Open Antigravity Settings
  2. Find “Terminal Sandboxing”
  3. Toggle “Enable Terminal Sandboxing”
  4. Configure network access permissions as needed

Why This Feature Matters

One user highlighted a common scenario:

“I need AI agents to work on code repositories while also organizing my computer files.”

This type of need is typical but carries significant risks without sandbox protection. The feature enables developers to:

  • Trust agents more freely – Work across multiple projects without constant worry
  • Safely vibe code – Give the IDE broader access when the sandbox protects the terminal
  • Isolate project work – Keep agent activity contained within specific directories

Early User Feedback

Not everything is perfect. Early adopters have reported some friction:

Deepak from DeepakNess noted that the sandbox “constantly asks me to run commands like sudo chown -R 501:20 "/Users/deepak/.npm"” and ultimately chose to disable it:

“The terminal sandboxing feature in Antigravity is annoying as hell… I am turning this feature off.”

This feedback highlights the trade-off between security and convenience. Sandbox restrictions can interfere with legitimate package manager operations and other development workflows.

The Bigger Picture

Terminal sandboxing represents a maturing of the agentic AI landscape. As we move from “AI as copilot” to “AI as autonomous agent,” safety mechanisms become essential.

This is particularly relevant given recent Chrome sandbox escape vulnerabilities (CVE-2025-2783, CVE-2025-13097) that demonstrate how difficult sandbox security truly is. Google’s decision to sandbox terminal commands shows they understand the stakes.

Best Practices

Until sandboxing is perfected, consider:

  1. Test in isolated environments – Use Docker containers or VMs for risky operations
  2. Review commands before execution – Many agents offer approval workflows
  3. Keep backups – Always have recovery options when working with autonomous agents
  4. Provide feedback – Report issues to help improve the feature

Looking Forward

The current implementation is macOS-only, with Linux coming soon. The default-off setting may change as the feature matures.

For now, terminal sandboxing in Google Antigravity is a significant step toward safer agentic AI development. It acknowledges the real risks of autonomous terminal access while providing a path toward making agents trustworthy development partners.

Sources

  • Google Antigravity Documentation - Sandbox Mode
  • Google Antigravity LinkedIn Announcement
  • Antigravity’s new Terminal Sandboxing is here | DeepakNess
  • Google Antigravity Safety Best Practices | Lumijure
  • CVE-2025-2783: Chrome Sandbox Escape | Sangfor

item.title: Vercel Agent Skills: Official Skills Collection for AI Coding Agents
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

As AI coding assistants become increasingly capable, developers are finding new ways to extend these agents with domain-specific knowledge. Vercel Labs has released an official collection of agent skills that encapsulates years of engineering expertise into machine-readable formats that AI agents can understand and apply.

The vercel-labs/agent-skills repository provides production-ready skills covering React optimization, web design guidelines, React Native patterns, and React composition patterns.

What Are Agent Skills?

Agent Skills are packaged instructions and scripts that extend AI coding agent capabilities. Unlike traditional documentation, skills are specifically formatted for LLM consumption, enabling agents to:

  • Parse and understand rules automatically
  • Apply patterns when writing or reviewing code
  • Detect relevant contexts and activate accordingly
  • Execute automation scripts when needed

Vercel’s skills follow the Agent Skills format, ensuring compatibility across multiple AI coding platforms.

Available Skills

1. React Best Practices

react-best-practices contains 40+ rules across 8 categories, distilled from Vercel Engineering’s decade of React and Next.js optimization experience.

Use when:

  • Writing new React components or Next.js pages
  • Implementing data fetching (client or server-side)
  • Reviewing code for performance issues
  • Optimizing bundle size or load times

Categories covered:

  • Eliminating waterfalls (Critical) - Parallel data fetching, prefetching
  • Bundle size optimization (Critical) - Tree shaking, code splitting, dynamic imports
  • Server-side performance (High) - Server components, streaming, caching
  • Client-side data fetching (Medium-High) - SWR/React Query patterns
  • Re-render optimization (Medium) - Memoization, useMemo, useCallback
  • Rendering performance (Medium) - Virtualization, CSS containment
  • JavaScript micro-optimizations (Low-Medium) - Event handlers, property access

2. Web Design Guidelines

web-design-guidelines audits UI code for 100+ rules covering accessibility, performance, and UX best practices.

Use when:

  • “Review my UI”
  • “Check accessibility”
  • “Audit design”
  • “Review UX”
  • “Check my site against best practices”

Categories covered:

  • Accessibility - ARIA labels, semantic HTML, keyboard navigation
  • Focus States - Visible focus indicators, focus-visible patterns
  • Forms - Autocomplete, validation, error handling
  • Animation - Prefers-reduced-motion, compositor-friendly transforms
  • Typography - Curly quotes, ellipsis, tabular nums
  • Images - Dimensions, lazy loading, alt text
  • Performance - Virtualization, layout thrashing prevention, preconnect
  • Navigation & State - URL reflects state, deep-linking
  • Dark Mode & Theming - Color-scheme, theme-color meta
  • Touch & Interaction - Touch-action, tap-highlight
  • Locale & i18n - Intl.DateTimeFormat, Intl.NumberFormat

3. React Native Guidelines

react-native-guidelines contains 16 rules across 7 sections covering mobile performance, architecture, and platform-specific patterns.

Use when:

  • Building React Native or Expo apps
  • Optimizing mobile performance
  • Implementing animations or gestures
  • Working with native modules or platform APIs

Categories covered:

  • Performance (Critical) - FlashList, memoization, heavy computation offloading
  • Layout (High) - Flex patterns, safe areas, keyboard handling
  • Animation (High) - Reanimated library, gesture handling
  • Images (Medium) - expo-image, caching, lazy loading
  • State Management (Medium) - Zustand patterns, React Compiler
  • Architecture (Medium) - Monorepo structure, import optimization
  • Platform (Medium) - iOS/Android specific patterns

4. Composition Patterns

composition-patterns helps avoid boolean prop proliferation through compound components, state lifting, and internal composition.

Use when:

  • Refactoring components with many boolean props
  • Building reusable component libraries
  • Designing flexible APIs
  • Reviewing component architecture

Patterns covered:

  • Extracting compound components
  • Lifting state to reduce prop drilling
  • Composing internals for flexibility
  • Building composable APIs

5. Vercel Deploy Claimable

vercel-deploy-claimable enables instant deployment to Vercel directly from AI conversations. Deployments are “claimable” - users can transfer ownership to their own Vercel account.

Use when:

  • “Deploy my app”
  • “Deploy this to production”
  • “Push this live”
  • “Deploy and give me the link”

Features:

  • Auto-detects 40+ frameworks from package.json
  • Returns preview URL (live site) and claim URL (transfer ownership)
  • Handles static HTML projects automatically
  • Excludes node_modules and .git from uploads

Installation

Install all Vercel Agent Skills with a single command:

The installer will automatically detect your installed AI agents and install skills using symlinks for easy updates. It supports 30+ AI coding agents.

Supported Agents

Vercel Agent Skills work with:

  • Claude Code (Primary target)
  • Cursor
  • VS Code
  • GitHub Copilot
  • Windsurf
  • OpenCode
  • Codex
  • Zed
  • And 20+ more agents

Usage Examples

Once installed, skills activate automatically when relevant contexts are detected:

React Performance Review

The agent will load react-best-practices and analyze:

  • Waterfall elimination opportunities
  • Bundle optimization potential
  • Re-render optimization needs
  • Server component migration opportunities

UI Accessibility Audit

The agent will load web-design-guidelines and audit:

  • ARIA label completeness
  • Keyboard navigation support
  • Focus state visibility
  • Semantic HTML usage

Mobile App Optimization

The agent will load react-native-guidelines and suggest:

  • FlashList implementation
  • Memoization strategies
  • Image optimization
  • Platform-specific patterns

Instant Deployment

The agent will:

  1. Package your project
  2. Detect framework from package.json
  3. Upload to Vercel
  4. Return preview and claim URLs

What Makes Vercel’s Skills Different?

Machine-Readable Format

Unlike traditional documentation that requires human interpretation, Vercel’s skills are structured specifically for LLM consumption. Rules are categorized by impact level and include clear trigger conditions.

Production-Tested Patterns

These aren’t theoretical guidelines—they’re distilled from real-world experience building and optimizing some of the web’s largest React applications at Vercel scale.

Prioritized by Impact

Rules are tagged by priority (Critical, High, Medium, Low), helping agents focus on changes that provide the most value first.

Comprehensive Coverage

With 100+ rules across web design and 40+ rules for React optimization, the skills cover edge cases that general AI training might miss.

Related Resources

  • GitHub Repository: vercel-labs/agent-skills
  • Agent Skills Format: agentskills.io
  • Vercel Blog: Introducing React Best Practices
  • DeepWiki: Skill Documentation

Conclusion

Vercel Agent Skills represent a significant step toward making AI coding assistants more practical for production development. By encoding engineering best practices in machine-readable formats, Vercel has created a bridge between human expertise and AI capability.

Whether you’re building a React app, optimizing performance, auditing accessibility, or deploying to production, these skills provide your AI assistant with the context it needs to deliver high-quality, production-ready code.

The skills are open source (MIT License) and continuously evolving. As AI coding assistants become more capable, expect to see more skills covering additional frameworks, languages, and specialized domains.

Tags: #AI #Vercel #React #AgentSkills #DeveloperTools

item.title: Google Antigravity Terminal Sandboxing: Kernel-Level Isolation for AI Agents
item.content:encoded:
Google Antigravity Terminal Sandboxing: Kernel-Level Isolation for AI Agents

Introduction

AI coding assistants have transformed how developers work, but they’ve also introduced a new category of risk: what happens when an AI agent executes a terminal command that affects your system beyond the project directory?

Google Antigravity’s new Terminal Sandboxing feature addresses this head-on with kernel-level isolation. When enabled, all terminal commands executed by the AI agent are restricted to a designated workspace, preventing accidental modifications to system files, user directories, or other sensitive locations.

This is a significant step forward for AI agent safety, particularly as these tools gain more autonomy and the ability to run complex multi-step operations.

Key Highlights

  • Kernel-level isolation via macOS Seatbelt (sandbox-exec) for robust security boundaries
  • File system restrictions limiting writes to workspace directory and essential system locations only
  • Independent network control — allow or deny network access while maintaining file system restrictions
  • Per-command bypass option in “Request Review” mode for flexibility when needed
  • Automatic activation in Secure Mode with network access denied by default
  • Linux support coming soon (currently macOS-only)

Prerequisites & Installation

Requirements

  • Google Antigravity (public preview — free account required)
  • macOS (current platform availability)
  • Antigravity desktop app or browser extension

Enabling Sandboxing

Sandboxing is disabled by default but can be toggled in Antigravity User Settings:

  1. Open Antigravity Settings
  2. Find the Terminal section
  3. Toggle “Enable Terminal Sandboxing” to turn sandboxing on or off
  4. Optionally toggle “Sandbox Allow Network” to control network access independently

When Secure Mode is enabled, sandboxing activates automatically with network access denied for maximum protection.

How Sandboxing Works

Under the Hood: Seatbelt

Google Antigravity leverages Apple’s Seatbelt sandboxing mechanism (sandbox-exec), which provides kernel-level isolation for processes. This is the same technology macOS uses to sandbox apps from the App Store.

When sandboxing is enabled:

  1. File System: The agent can only write to the designated workspace directory and read from essential system locations
  2. Network Access: Controlled independently via the “Sandbox Allow Network” toggle
  3. Violations: Any operation outside permitted boundaries is immediately blocked with a clear error message

What Gets Blocked

When an AI agent attempts an operation outside sandbox boundaries, users see explicit feedback:

Common blocked operations include:

  • Writing to directories outside the workspace
  • Deleting system files
  • Modifying user configuration files outside the project
  • Network requests (if network access is disabled)

Recommended Scenarios & Best Practices

For Individual Developers

Enable sandboxing when:

  • Working on critical systems where accidental changes could be costly
  • Letting AI agents run autonomously on complex tasks
  • Experimenting with new AI capabilities you don’t fully trust yet

Disable sandboxing when:

  • You need the agent to modify files across multiple project directories
  • Running trusted, well-understood commands
  • The friction of constant bypasses outweighs security benefits

For Teams and Enterprises

Recommended Configuration:

  • Enable Secure Mode for team environments
  • Keep sandboxing ON with network access DENIED by default
  • Use “Request Review” mode with per-command bypass for exceptions
  • Document approved bypass scenarios in team guidelines

Daily Workflow Examples

Scenario 1: Autonomous Refactoring

Scenario 2: Multi-Project Setup

Precautions & Troubleshooting

Common Issues

Issue: Commands that previously worked now fail

Solution: Check if the command tries to access files outside the workspace. Common culprits:

  • Package managers installing to global directories
  • Build tools that cache outside the project
  • Configuration files in ~/.config or similar

Issue: Agent terminates unexpectedly

Solution: Some workflows may break when sandboxed. Review the agent’s error logs for sandbox-related messages and adjust the workspace scope or use per-command bypass.

Issue: Network operations fail

Solution: If the agent needs network access (e.g., downloading dependencies), enable “Sandbox Allow Network” in settings while keeping file system restrictions.

FAQ

Q: Is sandboxing enabled by default?

A: No, sandboxing is currently opt-in, but this may change in future releases. Google Antigravity suggests that defaults may evolve as the feature matures.

Q: Can I run commands outside the sandbox?

A: Yes. In “Request Review” mode, you’ll see a “Bypass Sandbox” option for individual commands. This gives you flexibility without permanently disabling protection.

Q: Does this work on Linux or Windows?

A: Currently macOS-only due to the use of Apple’s Seatbelt mechanism. Linux support is explicitly listed as “coming soon.” Windows support details are not yet available.

Q: How does this compare to Docker or VMs?

A: Sandboxing is lighter-weight than full containerization. It’s a security boundary for terminal commands specifically, not a full environment isolation solution like Docker.

Comparison with Similar Products

FeatureGoogle AntigravityClaude CodeCursor
Terminal Sandboxing✅ Kernel-level (Seatbelt)❌ (planned)❌
File Operation Guard✅ Built-in✅ Built-in✅ Built-in
Network Control✅ Independent toggle⚠️ Partial❌
Per-Command Bypass✅ Request Review mode❌❌
Linux Support⚠️ Coming soon✅✅
macOS Support✅✅✅
Windows Support⚠️ TBD✅✅

Google Antigravity is the first major AI coding assistant to implement kernel-level terminal sandboxing, setting a new standard for AI agent safety.

User Reactions

The community response has been polarized:

Positive:

“Fantastic addition. You’re raising the bar every single day.”

Skeptical:

“I’ll stick with sudo gemini --yolo mode. Can we trust the models a bit more?”

Pragmatic:

“The important thing isn’t setting guardrails—it’s the model knowing what to run and where. But models aren’t perfect yet, so sandboxing is a necessary safety net.”

Some users have reported increased agent terminations after enabling sandboxing, suggesting that tighter security boundaries can expose fragile agent workflows.

Technical Deep Dive

Secure Mode Integration

When Secure Mode is enabled, the following configuration is automatically applied:

This configuration is designed for enterprise environments where data exfiltration and system modification are primary concerns.

Seatbelt Profile

While Google hasn’t published the exact Seatbelt profile being used, typical sandbox profiles include:

Future Directions

Given that Linux support is “coming soon,” Google Antigravity is likely implementing a cross-platform abstraction layer that will use:

  • Linux: Firejail, Bubblewrap, or seccomp-bpf
  • Windows: Windows Sandbox or AppContainer

Reference Links

  • Official Documentation: Sandboxing Terminal Commands
  • Google Antigravity: https://antigravity.google
  • Apple Seatbelt: macOS Sandbox Guide
  • Community Discussion: Hacker News

Conclusion

Google Antigravity’s terminal sandboxing represents a maturing of AI agent tooling. As AI assistants gain more capability, the need for robust safety boundaries becomes more pressing. Kernel-level isolation via Seatbelt provides exactly that—a security layer that operates below the application level, where even a misbehaving AI model cannot bypass it.

The feature is currently opt-in, but the trajectory is clear: as AI agents become more autonomous, sandboxing and similar safety mechanisms will move from optional to essential. For teams and individual developers alike, the question isn’t whether you need sandboxing—it’s whether you can afford not to have it.

Published: January 26, 2026

item.title: BMad Installation Guide: Key Points and Common Pitfalls
item.content:encoded:
TL;DR: Run bunx bmad-method@alpha install in your project (NOT home directory), keep TTS disabled, choose project-specific Codex prompts, and install all recommended modules (BMB, BMGD, BMM, CIS). Here’s what you need to know before installing BMad v6.0.0-alpha.23.

Introduction

BMad Method (Breakthrough Method for Agile AI-Driven Development) just hit alpha.23, bringing 67 workflows, 25 agents, and a completely reorganized installation experience. If you’re installing BMad for the first time—or upgrading from an older version—here are the key points to watch out for.

1. Installation Directory: Don’t Install in Home

First rule: don’t run the installer in your home directory.

The installer will confirm the resolved path and whether the directory is empty. This matters because BMad creates a _bmad folder with all its configuration, workflows, and agents.

2. AI Agents Selection: Recommended Tools Are Pre-Selected

During installation, you’ll be prompted to select AI tools to configure. The recommended tools are pre-selected for a reason—they’re the most stable and well-integrated options:

Recommended Tools (pre-selected):

  • Claude Code ⭐
  • Codex ⭐
  • Cursor ⭐
  • GitHub Copilot ⭐
  • Google Antigravity ⭐
  • OpenCode ⭐
  • Windsurf ⭐

Additional Tools (optional):

  • Atlassian Rovo Dev
  • Auggie CLI
  • Cline
  • Crush
  • Gemini CLI
  • iFlow CLI
  • Kilo Code
  • Kiro CLI
  • Qwen Code
  • Roo Code
  • Trae

If you’re just getting started, stick with the recommended tools. You can always add more later.

3. TTS: Keep It Disabled Unless You Need It

The installer asks if you want to enable Text-to-Speech:

Recommendation: Keep it disabled unless you’re specifically working alone on a local machine with audio output. TTS can surprise you with unexpected audio playback during collaborative sessions.

4. Module Selection: Install All Recommended Modules

BMad includes four main modules. Install all of them unless you have a specific reason to skip one:

  • BMB (BMad Builder): Agent, Workflow, and Module Builder
  • BMGD (BMad Game Development): Game development workflows
  • BMM (BMad Method): Core Agile-AI-Driven-Development methodology
  • CIS (Creative Innovation Suite): Creativity workflows

Each module extends BMad’s capabilities. You can accept defaults for all of them during installation.

5. Codex CLI: Choose Project-Specific Installation

When configuring Codex, you’ll be asked where to install prompts:

Choose Project-specific. This ties prompts to your project and enables version control. You’ll need to set CODEX_HOME to use these prompts:

6. Core Configuration: Use Your Team Name

The installer asks for your name and language preferences:

If you’re working with a team, use a team name instead of your personal name—this makes AI responses more contextually appropriate for group collaboration.

7. What Gets Installed

After a successful installation, you’ll have:

The installer generates:

  • 67 workflows for various development scenarios
  • 25 agents with specialized roles
  • 96 configuration files across all tools
  • Tool-specific configs for Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Copilot, and more

8. Upgrading from Older Versions

If you’re upgrading from a version older than alpha.21, you’ll need a fresh install:

  1. Remove the _bmad folder
  2. Reinstall using bunx bmad-method@alpha install

This is only necessary during the final push to beta (ETA January 18th, but already past as of this writing). After beta, upgrades should be smoother.

9. Verification and Next Steps

After installation:

  1. Check the _bmad folder exists in your project root
  2. Review the generated workflows in _bmad/bmm/workflows/
  3. Set up your IDE (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) to use the BMad patterns
  4. Read the docs at docs.bmad-method.org

Your first workflow should be quick-dev for rapid feature implementation or quick-spec for planning before coding.

Common Pitfalls to Avoid

PitfallSolution
Installing in home directoryAlways cd to project first
Skipping recommended toolsThey’re pre-selected for stability
Enabling TTS accidentallyKeep disabled unless solo work
Choosing global Codex promptsProject-specific enables version control
Using personal name for team projectsUse a team name instead

Closing

BMad v6.0.0-alpha.23 is a significant upgrade with better architecture, more workflows, and improved tool integration. The installation is straightforward once you know these key points.

If you run into issues, check the changelog or open an issue on GitHub.

What’s your experience with BMad installation? Any other tips to share?

item.title: BMad Quick Dev Workflow: Building a Game in 2.5 Hours
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

Last week, I decided to test the BMad Method’s Quick Dev workflow by building something from scratch: a stickman fighting game using Phaser 3. The goal was simple—see how much AI could autonomously handle, and where human intervention was actually needed.

Two and a half hours later, I had a complete playable game with:

  • Procedurally rendered stickman characters (no sprite assets needed)
  • Player controls (A/D move, W jump, J punch, K kick)
  • An AI opponent with approach/attack/retreat behavior
  • Health bars, hit detection, and knockback physics

Here’s how it went, step by step.

What is Quick Dev?

Quick Dev is BMad’s streamlined workflow for focused implementation tasks. It’s designed for situations where you either:

  • Have a tech-spec ready to execute, or
  • Know exactly what you want to build and want to dive straight in

The workflow uses a “step-file architecture”—each phase loads fresh to combat the “lost in the middle” problem that plagues long AI sessions. State persists through variables rather than context, allowing for marathon coding sessions without degradation.

The 6-Step Process

Step 1: Mode Detection

What happens: The workflow captures a baseline commit (for diff tracking), loads project context if available, and determines whether you’re working from a tech-spec or direct instructions.

Human intervention required:

When I ran /bmad:bmm:workflows:quick-dev with the prompt “build a stickman fighting game in Phaser 3,” the workflow analyzed the scope and presented three options:

My choice: I selected [e] - Execute directly. I wanted to see how far AI could go without formal planning.

Why this matters: This checkpoint prevents AI from committing to approaches that might be wrong. For larger projects (platforms, multi-system work), the workflow would have escalated me to full BMad Method planning.

Step 2: Context Gathering

What happens: The workflow analyzes the request to identify:

  • Files to modify (or create, for new projects)
  • Relevant patterns (from existing code or frameworks)
  • Dependencies (external libraries, internal modules)
  • A mental plan with tasks and acceptance criteria

What was presented:

Human intervention required: Confirmation to proceed. I could have adjusted the plan, but it looked solid.

Step 3: Execute

What happens: This is where the magic happens. The workflow implements tasks continuously—stopping only for blocking issues, not for approval between tasks.

Key principle: Do NOT halt for milestones. Execute all tasks in sequence.

What I watched happen:

The AI created a complete project structure:

Then it built each component:

  1. Project scaffolding - Vite + TypeScript + Phaser 3.80.1
  2. Stickman class - Procedural rendering using Phaser Graphics API. Each body part (head, torso, arms, legs) drawn as lines with joints
  3. Animation system - Pose-based animation with smooth transitions between idle, walk, jump, punch, kick states
  4. Combat system - Hitbox detection, damage calculation, knockback physics
  5. AI opponent - Simple state machine: approach when far, attack when close, retreat when health low

Human intervention required: None during execution. The workflow found a minor issue with jump animation (leg movement too subtle) and fixed it autonomously.

Total tokens used: ~155,000 Time: About 90 minutes of continuous work

Step 4: Self-Check

What happens: Before presenting results, the workflow self-audits:

  • Are all tasks complete?
  • Do tests pass?
  • Are acceptance criteria satisfied?
  • Were existing patterns followed?

What was presented:

Human intervention required: None—this is automatic. But if anything had failed, the workflow would have halted.

Step 5: Adversarial Review

What happens: The workflow constructs a diff of all changes and invokes an adversarial code reviewer—a separate agent that has only the diff, no context. This information asymmetry forces the reviewer to find real problems, not excuse things based on “what the AI was thinking.”

The process:

  1. Git diff from baseline commit
  2. New untracked files included
  3. Separate subagent reviews only the diff
  4. Findings categorized by severity and validity

What was found:

The adversarial reviewer found 3 findings:

IDSeverityValidityDescription
F1LowRealJump pose hip offset hardcoded to 2, should use direction
F2LowRealBody lean during jump too subtle (0.05), hard to notice
F3TrivialNoiseComment style inconsistency

Human intervention required: None yet—findings are presented for resolution in next step.

Step 6: Resolve Findings

What happens: You choose how to handle the findings:

Human intervention required: Decision on resolution approach.

My choice: [2] - Auto-fix. The findings were minor and the AI understood what needed fixing.

What was fixed:

  • F1: Changed hipOffsetX: dir * 2 to include both direction and air phase
  • F2: Increased body lean from 0.05 to 0.1 + air phase adjustment
  • F3: Skipped (noise)

Final result:

The Game That Was Built

Here’s what 2.5 hours produced:

The complete game is about 600 lines of TypeScript, with:

  • Smooth pose-based animation
  • Responsive keyboard controls
  • Basic AI that actually fights back
  • Health UI and game-over state

What AI Did Autonomously

PhaseTasksHuman Input
SetupProject initialization, dependency installation1 choice (execute mode)
AnalysisScope breakdown, task planning1 confirmation
Implementation600+ lines of code, all files0 interventions
TestingBuild verification, runtime testing0 interventions
ReviewAdversarial analysis, 3 findings found0 interventions
FixesApplied 2 fixes automatically1 choice (auto-fix)

Where Humans Are Still Needed

After this experiment, here’s where human input proved essential:

  1. Initial direction - The workflow can’t decide what to build
  2. Mode selection - Understanding whether formal planning is needed
  3. Complex decisions - Major architectural choices still benefit from human judgment
  4. Creative direction - Art style, game feel, these are human domains

Key Takeaways

  1. Step-file architecture works - 2.5 hours of continuous execution without context degradation
  2. Adversarial review finds real issues - The separate reviewer caught subtle bugs the main agent missed
  3. YOLO mode is viable for focused work - With proper guardrails, AI can execute autonomously
  4. Human attention is precious - The workflow preserves it for decision points, not implementation

Would I Use This Again?

Absolutely. For focused implementation tasks—features, refactorings, bug fixes—Quick Dev is significantly faster than traditional coding. The adversarial review catches issues I would have missed, and the step-file architecture enables marathon sessions.

Would I use it for a platform rewrite? No—that’s what the full BMad Method is for. But for a game, a feature, a service? Quick Dev delivers.

Resources

  • BMad Method Documentation: https://docs.bmad-method.org/
  • BMad GitHub: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD
  • Phaser 3: https://phaser.io/
  • Terminal log of this session: .terminal-logs/terminal_20260124_210313_1769259793512.log

This game was built using BMad Method v6.0.0-alpha.23 with Claude Opus 4.5. Total time from blank directory to playable game: approximately 2.5 hours.

item.title: Clawdbot: Your Personal AI Assistant That Lives in Your Favorite Apps
item.content:encoded:
Clawdbot: Your Personal AI Assistant That Lives in Your Favorite Apps

EXFOLIATE! EXFOLIATE! 🦞

Imagine having a personal AI assistant that doesn’t live in yet another app you need to download, but instead integrates directly into the messaging platforms you already use every day. That’s Clawdbot.

What is Clawdbot?

Clawdbot is an open-source personal AI assistant that you run on your own devices. It answers you on the channels you already use—WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack, Discord, Google Chat, Signal, iMessage, Microsoft Teams, and more. It has over 12,000 stars on GitHub and is growing rapidly.

Unlike ChatGPT or Claude’s web interfaces, Clawdbot meets you where you already are. No need to switch apps or open a browser. Just send a message like you would to a friend.

Why This Matters

Most AI assistants live in walled gardens. They want you to use their app, their interface, their ecosystem. Clawdbot takes a different approach:

  • Your data stays yours — Self-hosted means your conversations never leave your infrastructure
  • Your choice of interface — Use WhatsApp on mobile, Slack at work, Discord with friends
  • Always available — Runs 24/7 on your server
  • Extensible — Build custom skills and automate your workflows

Key Features

Multi-Channel Inbox

Clawdbot supports 15+ messaging platforms:

  • WhatsApp — Most popular option, works via Baileys
  • Telegram — Fast and reliable via grammY
  • Slack — Perfect for work environments
  • Discord — Great for gaming communities
  • Google Chat — Workspace integration
  • Signal — Privacy-focused option
  • iMessage — macOS only, native Apple experience
  • Microsoft Teams — Enterprise support
  • BlueBubbles — Extension for iMessage
  • Matrix — Decentralized messaging
  • Zalo — Popular in Vietnam
  • WebChat — Browser-based interface

Voice Wake + Talk Mode

On macOS, iOS, and Android, Clawdbot supports always-on voice activation:

  • Say “Hey Clawd” to wake it up
  • Continuous conversation mode
  • ElevenLabs integration for natural speech
  • Push-to-talk overlay on mobile

Live Canvas

A unique feature that lets the AI create visual workspaces you can control:

  • Agent-driven visual workspace
  • A2UI (Agent-to-UI) push and reset
  • Real-time updates from your assistant
  • Perfect for brainstorming and planning

Browser Control

Clawdbot can control a dedicated browser instance:

  • Automated web browsing
  • Screenshot capture
  • Form filling
  • Research tasks

Multi-Agent Routing

Run multiple isolated agents for different purposes:

  • Separate sessions for different contexts
  • Group chat isolation
  • Per-channel routing
  • Activation modes (mention or always-on)

Getting Started

Prerequisites

  • Node.js ≥22
  • npm, pnpm, or bun
  • For cloud deployment: a VPS (optional, ~$5/month)

Installation

The recommended installation uses the onboarding wizard:

The wizard guides you through:

  1. Gateway setup
  2. Workspace configuration
  3. Channel connection
  4. Skills installation

Configuration

A minimal ~/.clawdbot/clawdbot.json:

For Anthropic Claude, configure OAuth:

The recommended model is Claude Opus 4.5 for its long-context strength and better resistance to prompt injection.

Channel Setup

WhatsApp:

Then scan the QR code with your phone.

Telegram:

Set your bot token:

Discord:

Chat Commands

Once Clawdbot is running, use these commands in any channel:

CommandDescription
/statusShow session status and model info
/new or /resetReset the conversation
/compactSummarize and compress context
/think <level>Set thinking level (off/minimal/low/medium/high)
`/verbose onoff`
/usageShow token usage and costs

Architecture

The Gateway is the control plane. It manages sessions, channels, tools, and events. Everything connects to it via WebSocket.

Security

Clawdbot connects to real messaging surfaces, so it treats inbound DMs as untrusted input by default.

Default DM policy (pairing mode):

  • Unknown senders receive a pairing code
  • The bot doesn’t process their message
  • You must explicitly approve with:

For public access, explicitly opt in:

Run clawdbot doctor to check for risky configurations.

Skills and Automation

Clawdbot has a skills platform that lets you extend its capabilities:

  • Bundled skills — Built-in functionality
  • Managed skills — Community-maintained
  • Workspace skills — Your custom automations

ClawdHub is a skill registry where you can discover and install new skills.

Example skills include:

  • Email triage and drafting
  • Calendar management
  • Smart home control
  • Browser automation
  • Research and summarization

Deployment Options

Local Development

Docker

Cloud VPS

Run the Gateway on a small Linux instance (~$5/month). Use Tailscale Serve or SSH tunnels for remote access.

The Gateway handles channel connections and exec tools. Device nodes (macOS/iOS/Android) run device-local actions via node.invoke.

Community

Clawdbot is built by Peter Steinberger (@steipete) and a growing community:

  • GitHub: https://github.com/clawdbot/clawdbot
  • Discord: https://discord.gg/clawd
  • Documentation: https://docs.clawd.bot/
  • Showcase: https://docs.clawd.bot/start/showcase

Special thanks to Mario Zechner for pi-mono, the agent runtime that powers Clawdbot.

Comparison with Alternatives

FeatureClawdbotChatGPT AppClaude AppSiri
Self-hosted✅❌❌❌
Multi-channel✅ 15+❌❌❌
Voice wake✅✅✅✅
Browser control✅❌❌❌
Custom skills✅❌❌❌
Visual canvas✅❌❌❌
Data ownership✅❌❌⚠️

FAQ

Q: Does Clawdbot work on Windows?

A: Yes, via WSL2 (Windows Subsystem for Linux). This is the recommended approach for Windows users.

Q: What’s the minimum server requirement?

A: The cheapest VPS options (~$4-5/month) are sufficient. For personal use, a 2GB RAM instance works well.

Q: Can I use it without programming knowledge?

A: Yes! The onboarding wizard (clawdbot onboard) guides you through everything. No coding required for basic usage.

Q: Which AI model should I use?

A: Claude Opus 4.5 is recommended for its long-context capabilities and better prompt-injection resistance. However, any model supported by Anthropic or OpenAI works.

Q: Is my data safe?

A: Clawdbot is self-hosted, so your data stays on your infrastructure. The only external connection is to the AI model provider (Anthropic/OpenAI).

Q: Can I run multiple agents?

A: Yes! Clawdbot supports multi-agent routing. You can have separate agents for different contexts, channels, or purposes.

What’s Next?

Clawdbot represents a different vision for personal AI assistants—one that prioritizes:

  • Privacy through self-hosting
  • Flexibility through multi-channel support
  • Extensibility through skills
  • Control through open source

If you’re tired of AI assistants locked in corporate walled gardens, Clawdbot might be exactly what you’ve been looking for.

Resources

  • GitHub Repository — Source code and issues
  • Documentation — Comprehensive guides
  • Getting Started — Installation guide
  • MacStories Review — In-depth review
  • Discord Community — Chat with other users

Built by Peter Steinberger and the Clawdbot community. EXFOLIATE! 🦞

item.title: Cursor + Claude Code: How to Build an Agentic Workspace for Non-Technical People
item.content:encoded:
Cursor + Claude Code: How to Build an Agentic Workspace for Non-Technical People

Claude Code has dominated the developer timeline in recent weeks—but almost exclusively among technical users. As a non-engineer, how do you use Claude Code, and why would you want to?

Unless you’re building new UI (like personal software), the best way for a prosumer to use Claude Code is through Cursor, giving it access to actions across Chrome and your local machine. Used this way, it’s the most powerful agent I’ve tested that can take action both on your browser and on your files.

This differs from Claude itself (mostly chat with limited agentic capability via Connectors), and from Manus and ChatGPT Agent. Both have browser agents, but ChatGPT Agent runs in a cloud sandbox and can’t access your local files or your actual browser sessions, while Manus can control the browser but not local files. They also tend to be slower—there’s VM spin-up time, network latency on every action, and when they go off track you can’t course-correct until they’re done.

I’ve also found Claude Code used in Chrome to be more powerful for some use cases than Claude Cowork, though Cowork can be more efficient—more on this below.

What Makes This Setup Different

ToolBrowser AccessLocal FilesReal-time ControlSpeed
Claude Code + Cursor✅ Playwright MCP✅ Full workspace✅ Step-by-step approvalFast
Claude Cowork✅ Vision-based❌ Cloud only❌ AutonomousMedium
ChatGPT Agent✅ Cloud sandbox❌ No❌ AutonomousSlow
Manus✅ Browser control❌ No❌ AutonomousSlow

The key differentiator: Claude Code in Cursor gives you precision control. You see every action before it happens, catch mistakes immediately, and can guide the agent in real-time.

Account Setup

First, download and sign up for a few things:

  • GitHub – Create an account. Only used for identity and permissions—no git knowledge required
  • Claude – Create an account. You’ll need Pro ($20/month) or Max ($100/month) for Claude Code
  • Cursor – Create an account and download

Next, go to the Claude web app, click “Code” in the upper left corner, and connect your GitHub.

Activate Claude Code

Once GitHub is connected, you’ll see a terminal interface. This is Claude Code. First time setup will walk you through authentication and preferences.

For full browser control, install locally:

Connect Claude Code to Cursor

Claude Code is a supervised agent that reads/writes files in a chosen folder. Cursor is the UI that lets you watch and steer it.

The easiest method: the Claude Code extension.

  1. In Cursor, click Extensions (left sidebar or Cmd+Shift+X)
  2. Search “Claude Code” and install by Anthropic
  3. Click the Claude icon in your sidebar
  4. Open any folder—this becomes your “workspace” where Claude can see and edit files

Claude can now see all workspace files, edit them, create new ones, and run commands—all through natural language.

Set Up Browser Control (Playwright MCP)

This is where it gets powerful. Give Claude the ability to control Chrome—clicking buttons, filling forms, reading pages, taking screenshots.

In your Claude Code session (inside Cursor’s terminal):

Select “Add MCP Server” → Choose “Playwright” → Follow prompts.

Once set up, you can ask Claude things like:

  • “Go to my Google Calendar and find all meetings next week with external attendees”
  • “Read this PDF contract and summarize the key terms into a new doc on my Desktop”

Claude Code uses Playwright’s accessibility tree to navigate—reading structured data about page elements rather than taking screenshots. I’ve found this faster and more reliable than Cowork’s vision-based approach.

This works best when you think of Claude as a junior operator sitting next to you, not an autonomous bot you walk away from. You’ll need to log in to various platforms (Twitter, LinkedIn, etc.) the first time as Playwright spins up a fresh browser.

What You Can Actually Do

With an agentic workspace spanning browser and local machine, here’s what becomes possible:

  • Research + organize: “Go to Crunchbase, look up these 10 companies, and put their funding info into a spreadsheet in my Documents folder”
  • Content creation: “Help me edit this Twitter article I’m drafting” (yes, this article was edited with Claude Code)
  • Data entry: “Fill out this expense report form with the receipts I have in my Downloads folder”
  • Email cleanup: “Go to my Gmail, find all the newsletters I haven’t opened in 3 months, and unsubscribe from them”

Why Not Just Use Claude Cowork?

For many users and use cases, Claude Cowork is the better choice. It’s simpler (just the Claude desktop app), uses your existing Chrome sessions, and runs autonomously for long tasks. If you want simple file organization or fire-and-forget browser tasks, start with Cowork.

But Claude Code in Cursor is worth the extra setup for prosument who want fine-grained control:

  • Quota efficiency: Cowork burned through my allowance fast. It runs autonomously, often taking more steps than necessary. Throughout the day, this adds up.
  • Real-time control: You see every action. If it’s about to make a mistake, you catch it immediately—not after it’s reorganized 500 files wrong.
  • Precision: For tasks that must be done exactly right, watching step-by-step beats hoping Cowork interpreted correctly.
  • Terminal access: If your workflows involve scripts, git, or command-line tools, Claude Code can run them. Cowork can’t.
  • Reliability: Cowork can stall mid-task on complex workflows. While writing this article, I asked Cowork to organize screenshots on my desktop and it completely stalled—Claude Code handled it instantly.

I’d recommend trying both—your ideal workflow might involve using Cowork for autonomous tasks and Claude Code + Cursor for precision work.

Reference Links

  • Claude Code
  • Cursor
  • Playwright MCP
  • Original thread by @omooretweets

item.title: Goodbye Vibe Coding: Spec-Driven Development Frameworks for Professional AI Software Development
item.content:encoded:
Goodbye Vibe Coding: Spec-Driven Development Frameworks for Professional AI Software Development

The Problem with Vibe Coding

“Vibe coding” - writing code “by feeling” and hoping the AI guesses your intentions - seemed efficient at first. You describe what you want, the AI generates code, you copy-paste. Fast, right?

But this apparent speed hides structural problems that inevitably emerge:

  • Context rot: As conversations lengthen, the AI “forgets” previous decisions and generates code inconsistent with what was done before
  • Emergent architecture (in the worst sense): Without an overall vision, code grows chaotically and quickly becomes impossible to maintain
  • Lack of traceability: Impossible to understand why certain technical decisions were made
  • Onboarding nightmare: New team members have no references to understand the system

Enter Spec-Driven Development

Spec-Driven Development (SDD) is the professional answer. A methodology where specifications become first-class executable artifacts. You write the “spec” (structured documentation) first, then the AI generates code that respects that contract.

The spec becomes the source of truth for developers and AI agents, eliminating the ambiguities of vibe coding.

According to Thoughtworks, SDD is one of the most important engineering practices in recent years.

BMAD Method: The Enterprise Powerhouse

The BMAD Method (Build More, Architect Dreams) is the most complete SDD framework available today. With 21 specialized AI agents, over 50 guided workflows, and intelligence adaptive to project scale, BMAD simulates an entire agile team with specialized roles.

Key Features

  • Multi-agent: Business Analyst, Product Manager, System Architect, Scrum Master, Developer, UX Designer, QA and others
  • Scale-adaptive: Automatically adapts from bug fixes to enterprise systems
  • Open source: 100% free, no paywall
  • Cross-domain: Works for software, creative writing, business strategy, wellness

Installation

Typical BMAD Workflow

  1. Analysis: Analyst agent gathers requirements and context
  2. Planning: PM and Architect create PRD and architecture documents
  3. Stories: Scrum Master converts docs into detailed dev stories
  4. Implementation: Developer implements following the stories
  5. QA: Quality Assurance verifies the code

Documented Results

BMAD has been tested in enterprise contexts with measurable results:

Banking System Migration: A team of 3 developers used BMAD to convert a 50,000 LOC COBOL system to Java Spring Boot. Result: 40% reduction in integration time, zero critical regressions.

Fintech MVP: An Italian startup developed a wealth management platform with MiFID II compliant documentation. Certification obtained on first attempt, estimated €80,000 savings in compliance consulting.

Links: GitHub | Documentation

GSD (Get Shit Done): The Anti-Bureaucracy Framework

GSD by TACHES was born as a reaction to perceived complexity. As the creator says: “I’m a solo developer. I don’t write code - Claude Code does. But other SDD tools seem to complicate everything with sprint ceremonies.”

Philosophy: Hidden Complexity, Exposed Simplicity

The complexity is in the system, not in your workflow.

  • Maximum 3 tasks per plan: Each plan runs in a fresh sub-agent with 200k clean tokens
  • Git-centric: Each task is a separate commit, revertable and traceable
  • Zero context rot: Combats context loss through isolated sub-agents

Installation

How GSD Works

GSD uses a 3-phase funnel:

  1. PLANNING prompt: Performs gap analysis and generates prioritized TODO list
  2. BUILDING prompt: Implements tasks, runs tests, commits
  3. Loop: Repeats until completion

Documented Results

Alex Lavaee’s Experiment: A solo developer produced 100,000 lines of code in 2 weeks. Every commit traceable, git bisect finds exact bugs, zero context management overhead.

Enterprise Adoption: Used by engineers at Amazon, Google, Shopify, and Webflow.

Link: GitHub

Ralph Loop: The Autonomous Iteration Method

The Ralph Wiggum Loop (or simply Ralph Loop), created by Geoffrey Huntley, represents a paradigm shift. Instead of maintaining perfect context, it embraces fresh starts and lets git be the memory layer.

Core Concept: Git As Memory

Ralph runs AI agents in automated loops until specifications are met. Each iteration spawns a new agent process with a clean context window. The agent reads specs from disk, takes a task, implements it, and exits.

Unlike exit-hook plugins that force the same session to continue (causing context overflow), this approach cleanly terminates and restarts between tasks.

Installation

How It Works

Claude Code automatically: works on the task, attempts to exit, the stop hook blocks the exit, the stop hook reinserts the same prompt, repeats until completion.

Documented Results

DevOps Automation: A DevOps team implemented Ralph Loop in CI/CD. When tests fail, Ralph automatically receives the report, analyzes code, proposes fixes, and reruns tests.

Results after 3 months:

  • 67% of regression bugs resolved automatically
  • Average fix time reduced from 4 hours to 23 minutes
  • Zero manual interventions for 40% of failed builds

Links: Official Plugin | Smart Ralph

GitHub Spec Kit: The Open Source Standard

GitHub Spec Kit is GitHub’s open source toolkit for Spec-Driven Development. It provides a structured process to bring SDD into workflows with GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, and Gemini CLI.

Commands

The workflow follows 5 phases:

  1. /speckit.constitution - Set up principles and coding standards
  2. /speckit.specify - Define the feature with requirements
  3. /speckit.plan - Plan with architecture and dependencies
  4. /speckit.tasks - Generate ordered task list
  5. /speckit.implement - Execute tasks, create files, run tests

Documented Results

Several open source projects adopted Spec Kit for managing external contributions. Contributors first create a spec of the proposed feature, which is reviewed before writing code.

Observed results:

  • 60% reduction in PRs rejected for “architecture misalignment”
  • Review time halved thanks to pre-existing documentation
  • New contributor onboarding accelerated by 70%

Links: GitHub | Blog Post

Other Notable SDD Frameworks

Kiro (Amazon)

Amazon’s agentic IDE based on Code OSS, designed from the ground up around an SDD workflow. Explicitly in contrast with vibe coding: spec → design → tasks → implementation.

Link: kiro.dev

Tessl

The only tool that explicitly aspires to a spec-anchored approach. A Tessl spec can serve as the main artifact, with code marked // GENERATED FROM SPEC - DO NOT EDIT.

Link: tessl.io

cc-sdd

Brings Kiro-style commands enforcing a structured requirements→design→tasks workflow. Supports Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Gemini CLI, and Windsurf.

Link: GitHub

Framework Comparison

FrameworkIdeal ForComplexityAgentsEnterprise Ready
BMAD MethodEnterprise teams, complex projectsHigh21+Yes
GSDSolo developers, fast iterationLow1-3Partial
Ralph LoopAutonomous tasks, CI/CD automationMedium1-7Yes
GitHub Spec KitOpen standard, mixed teamsMediumVariableYes
KiroAWS ecosystem, greenfield projectsMedium-HighIntegratedYes

When SDD Makes Sense

Not all projects benefit from Spec-Driven Development. For a small bug fix, the workflow is “like using a sledgehammer to crack a nut.”

Use SDD When:

  • Greenfield projects: New applications from scratch
  • Complex features: Functionality requiring architecture
  • Distributed teams: Where documentation is critical
  • Strict compliance: Regulated sectors (finance, healthcare)
  • Knowledge preservation: When staff turnover is high

Skip SDD For:

  • Small bug fixes
  • Quick prototypes
  • Large legacy codebases (retroactive SDD is impractical)
  • Minor UI changes

Criticisms and Limitations

SDD has its critics. Some argue it “revives the old idea of heavy documentation before coding - an echo of the Waterfall era.”

Valid concerns:

  • Diminishing returns: SDD shines for new projects, but specs can diverge from code reality as applications grow
  • Legacy codebases: Retroactive SDD is often impractical
  • Initial overhead: Requires time investment for setup and training
  • Learning curve: Teams must learn new workflows

Getting Started

For Solo Developers

Start with GSD on a personal project to understand the basic concepts.

For Teams

Evaluate BMAD with a limited pilot project. The multi-agent approach mirrors existing agile roles.

For Enterprise

Consider GitHub Spec Kit for its integration with existing GitHub ecosystem.

Resources

  • BMAD Method
  • GSD (Get Shit Done)
  • Ralph Wiggum Plugin
  • GitHub Spec Kit
  • Kiro
  • Tessl
  • Thoughtworks on SDD

Remember: The right tool depends on your context. Start small, validate, then scale. Vibe coding might feel fast, but professional software development demands structure. Spec-Driven Development brings that structure without sacrificing the power of AI assistance.
